Tafseer:
The nature of the incident involving Abdullah bin Umm Maktum:
The subject of 'abas' (he frowned) is not explicitly mentioned here, but the subsequent verses clarify that the subject is the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). All commentators agree that 'al-a'ma' (the blind man) refers to Abdullah bin Umm Maktum. He was a loyal and blind companion. Narrations state that one day, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was speaking to one or a group of the leaders of the Quraysh. He was presenting Islam to them, and they were raising their objections and doubts, when Abdullah bin Umm Maktum arrived, and being unable to assess the sensitivity of the occasion, entered the assembly. His untimely arrival displeased the Prophet. The cause of this displeasure, obviously, was not that he was poor or blind—who could appreciate the poor and blind more than the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—but the Prophet feared that the opportunity to bring these wild leaders closer to Islam might be lost. He feared they might run away, saying: 'Since you have gathered such poor and destitute individuals around you, who would compromise his honor by sitting in your assembly?'
The sensitive temperament of the leaders of the Quraysh: It should noted that among the objections the arrogant leaders of the Quraysh had against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), a major one was that his companions were poor and destitute. They used this as an argument against his prophethood. Furthermore, the Prophet might have been anxious: 'They, in their pride, might insult or hurt my beloved companion, causing further unpleasantness.'
This incident, which occurred completely by chance, was used by Allah Almighty as a means to teach the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): make those companions who seek reform and attend your assembly with eagerness and desire the primary focus of your attention; do not follow too much those who are indifferent and wish for you to please them.
Instruction to ignore the arrogant: In the previous Surahs, it was explained that initially, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) made a special effort to call the leaders of the Quraysh. One reason was that initially, as is clear from: (And warn your closest kindred) (Ash-Shu'ara, 26:214), he was commanded to address them. Second, these people possessed the leadership of the entire Arabs; it was expected that if they accepted the call, the path to its success would open across Arabia. Thus, for some time, the Prophet focused his entire effort on them, and despite their extreme arrogance, mockery, and hostility, he continued to call them. But when their arrogance grew too much and it became clear that they were not going to accept any reform, and the time spent on them was causing a neglect of the poor Muslims who had believed and deserved his attention more, he was stopped from following them, and this incident of Abdullah bin Umm Maktum provided a highly suitable occasion.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1 and 2 was combined in the original text.]

The nature of the correction to the Prophet:
While the preceding verses merely reported an incident without clarifying whom it involved or what its purpose was, here 'yudreeka' (what can make you know) clarifies that the incident involves you, and you are warned: 'In searching for the lost sheep, you occasionally go so far that the care of the sheep in the flock is neglected.'
Not mentioning the addressee in the first part creates a question in the reader's mind to find out whom it involves, but since the addressee is not explicit, there is no reason for him to feel any personal concern. Thus, while the question must have arisen in the Prophet's mind, since the address was not direct, it did not cause much concern. Conversely, if the address had been explicit, this correction would have been very severe, because it would have specified that you were corrected because you frowned upon the arrival of a blind man, whereas the actual situation, as clarified, was not that.
The nature of lapses committed by prophets: The address is explicit here because the matter for which he was corrected is explained. This is not a neglect of duty, but exceeding the desired limit in performing the duty. We repeatedly clarify that when prophets commit a lapse, it is not out of self-interest, but rather because they exceed the desired limit in pursuing their Lord's pleasure. For example, when Allah Almighty called Moses to Mount Tur to receive the Shariah, He appointed a specific date, but Moses, in his eagerness, could not wait and arrived before the date. When corrected for his haste, he excused himself: 'O my Lord, I hastened to You to please You.' Such a lapse, obviously, is motivated by a noble desire, but since the prophets are the perfect balance of truth and justice, Allah Almighty corrects even such lapses to keep the balance perfect.
The lapse for which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is corrected here is of this nature. Since he expected that their acceptance of faith would open the path across Arabia, his dedication to this task grew so much that he cared neither for his own rest nor for how they treated him and his call. Indeed, this dedication raised the risk that he might find little time to fulfill his responsibility of training the poor Muslims who had believed. Regarding this, the Qur'an repeatedly corrected him in a highly affectionate manner, informing him that he had taken a heavier burden than what Allah had placed on him: 'Do not exhaust yourself over them. Your duty was only to communicate the message, which you have done; now, there is no need to please them.' Under these circumstances, this incident of Abdullah bin Umm Maktum occurred, which resulted in the revelation of a decisive Surah. Reflect on these verses in the light of this introduction.
'And what can make you know, [O Muhammad], that perhaps he might be purified' (wa ma yudreeka la'allahu yazzakka) - That is: 'You felt his arrival was heavy because you feared it might offend the pride of those leaders and drive them away, but you might ignore a sincere seeker to please those who will still not listen. How can neglecting a companion worthy of training be permissible for the sake of those unworthy individuals?'
The primary deserving of the Prophet's attention: This shows that the primary objective of a messenger is the purification (tazkiyah) of people. Those who seek purification are the primary deserving of his attention and care. Others, no matter how important they appear, have no value in his mission if they lack the desire for reform.
The two qualities of a sincere seeker: Two qualities of a sincere seeker are mentioned here: first, he seeks purification; second, he benefits from the reminder.
These are the qualities of the true members of the Prophet's training assembly, who fell into two categories: those who sought guidance regarding some specific matter of self-reform, and those who attended the assembly to benefit from whatever the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) spoke. 'perhaps he might be purified' points to the first category, and 'Or be reminded and the remembrance would benefit him' (aw yadhdhakkaru fa-tanfa'ahu-dh-dhikra) points to the second. Both are ways of seeking knowledge, and mentioning both is to show: whoever wishes to attend the Prophet's assembly should do so for one of these purposes, and they alone are worthy of his attention. As for those who wish to be pleased, they should remain in their homes and await their end.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 3 and 4 was combined in the original text.]

The primary correction:
This is the primary correction given to the Prophet on this occasion: those who seek reform and fear presenting themselves before God should be the primary deserving of your attention; but you are dedicating your days and nights to those who are indifferent, whereas if they do not reform themselves, you are not responsible. Your responsibility is for those who attend your assembly with eagerness, whom you are neglecting.
Clarifying some points:
First, while the correction is apparently directed to the Prophet, its actual focus is on those unworthy leaders of the Quraysh from whom no goodness could be expected. Therefore, it was necessary for him to ignore them and focus on the poor Muslims who had believed and deserved his training.
Second, the Prophet was not corrected for any neglect of duty, but for taking a heavier burden than what Allah had placed on him. This is an affectionate correction, similar to:
(Perhaps, [O Muhammad], you would kill yourself with grief that they will not be believers) (Ash-Shu'ara, 26:3),
and other similar verses.
Third, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is guided to the reality that the true wealth of Islam lies in those poor who fear God, not in those rich whose hearts are devoid of His fear. Therefore, focus on those who are worthy, and do not waste your time on those in whom no goodness remains.
A reality-reflecting metaphor for this correction: Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained the focus of this correction through an excellent metaphor, writing:
'Compare this to a highly dedicated shepherd. One fat sheep of his flock strays into the forest. He goes to search for it, finding its footprints and hearing its sound from a distance. Hoping to succeed, he goes far, neglecting his flock for a while. When he returns, the master corrects him: "Why did you leave the entire flock for the sake of a single mad sheep? You should have left it for the wolf to eat, for she deserved it." In whom did the correction focus? On the shepherd or on the lost sheep? The exact same is the case here. While the correction is apparently directed to the Prophet, its actual focus is on the opponents. For the Prophet, this correction contains highly affectionate compassion.' (Tafseer of Surah 'Abasa by Imam Farahi).

'Tasadda' is originally 'tatasaddadu', derived from the root 'sadad', which means parallel and opposite. The grammatical change is in accordance with the rules of Arabic. The meaning is: you focus on those who are indifferent, trying to please them, whereas Allah has not placed this burden on you.

'And no [blame] is upon you if he is not purified' (wa ma 'alayka alla yazzakka) - That is: your responsibility was only to warn and communicate, after which you are absolved. You are not responsible for forcing them to believe. This theme has appeared in various places to clarify that you should not take a heavier burden than what your Lord has placed on you. If these unfortunate individuals do not seek reform, leave them to their destiny.

'But as for him who came to you running' (wa amma man ja'aka yas'a) - 'Sa'y' means performing a task with eagerness, desire, and energy; running is not necessary. 'Then hasten to the remembrance of Allah' means: hasten to the remembrance of Allah with eagerness. In the verse, this word is used in this sense—referring to those who attend your assembly with eagerness, just as a thirsty person hastens to a spring.

'While he fears [Allah]' (wa huwa yakhsha) - This is in contrast to: 'But as for him who thinks himself self-sufficient' (amma man-istaghna). That is: some are so lost in their worldly pursuits that they never worry about the life after this world. Others fear presenting themselves before God, and they alone can be expected to listen to your words and cherish them, not the first group; yet, you are striving to make an impact on stones (i.e., those who do not listen), while you find little time for those who are worthy of your attention.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9 and 10 was combined in the original text.]

The primary responsibility of the Messenger:
'No!' (Kalla) - there is no need to please such unworthy individuals in this manner. This Qur'an is indeed a reminder. Whoever wishes may benefit from it, and whoever rejects it should be prepared to face the end. The meaning is: the Prophet's responsibility is to communicate this reminder, not to force it upon their hearts. His responsibility is to warn, not to please everyone!
In 'Indeed, it is' (innaha), the reference of the pronoun is 'dhikra' (reminder) in verse 4. In 'remembers it' (dhakarahu), the reference is also the same, but the pronoun is masculine because it refers to the meaning. Since both 'dhikra' and 'tadhkirah' refer to the Qur'an, using the masculine pronoun clarifies their actual meaning.
This clarifies the reality we noted above: if there is any correction to the Prophet, it is an affectionate correction, questioning why he carried a heavier burden than what his Lord placed on him.

After 'So whoever wills may remember it' (fa-man-sha'a dhakarahu), a portion is omitted based on context. If unpacked, the full sentence is: 'So whoever wills may benefit from this reminder, and whoever wishes may remain deaf.' Elsewhere, He states:
(So whoever wills—let him believe; and whoever wills—let him disbelieve) (Al-Kahf, 18:29).

Describing the grandeur of the Qur'an:
This further explains the instruction to ignore the arrogant leaders of the Quraysh. The meaning is: just as it is not fitting for you to bow before these arrogant individuals, this klam (speech) also is not something to be presented with pleading; rather, it is highly noble, elevated, and pure. This is not a cheap product that you must sell by flattering customers; rather, you are sharing the precious gems of Allah's protected treasury. If they do not appreciate it, they harm only themselves, falling into eternal loss.
'Recorded in honored sheets' (fi suhufim-mukarramah) - This describes the high origin and status of this klam. 'Fi suhuf' is originally 'huwa fi suhuf' (it is in sheets), where the subject is omitted, which is grammatically accepted when describing attributes.
'Sahifah' means a written sheet. In its plural form, it is sometimes used for a book. Here, it refers to the Preserved Tablet (Lawh-i-Mahfudh). 'Mukarramah' means it is a precious and protected treasury, which Allah's angels preserve with care. This points to the reality that not everyone has access to it, nor is everyone worthy of receiving it; rather, Allah blesses with it whom He wills, and He has blessed you with it, so share this blessing only with those who are worthy. Do not throw these pearls before the unworthy. This teaching also appeared in the Gospels as an effective metaphor, and we have cited it.

'Exalted and purified' (marfoo'atim-mutahharah) - These two attributes also explain its noble status. 'Marfoo'ah' points to its height in terms of both meaning and status, described elsewhere as:
(And indeed it is, in the Mother of the Book with Us, exalted and full of wisdom) (Az-Zukhruf, 43:4).
'Mutahharah' points to the reality that the Qur'an is completely secure from the reach of devils and corrupt spirits, as stated:
(In a Register well-protected, which none can touch except the purified) (Al-Waqi'ah, 56:78-79).

The attributes of the bearers of this Book:
'[Carried] by the hands of messenger-angels, noble and dutiful' (bi-aydee safaratin kiramim-bararah) - This describes the attributes of the angels in whose custody Allah has preserved this Book. We cited the verse: (which none can touch except the purified). What was stated there in a negative style is stated here in a positive style. The meaning is: corrupt spirits have no reach to this Book, but it is in the custody of pure angels who are noble and dutiful.
'Safarah' is the plural of 'safir', which means scribe or reader. 'Safar' is used in the sense of both writing and reading. Its analysis shows that while its root meaning is writing, it extended to reading and explaining.

The word 'kiram' points to their high status and noble character—showing that they are so noble that no betrayal (khiyanah) can be feared from them. They can neither alter it themselves, nor can the devils find any opportunity to reach it.
'Bararah' is the plural of 'barr', which means obedient, loyal, and fulfilling responsibilities. This attribute is added to highlight their trustworthiness—for example, He states:
(The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down) (Ash-Shu'ara, 26:193),
and elsewhere:
(Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger, possessed of power and with the Owner of the Throne, secure [in position], obeyed there [in the heavens] and trustworthy.) (At-Takwir, 81:19-21).
Describing the attributes of the Qur'an and its guardians, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed: this Qur'an is not something to be presented with pleading; rather, its call should be delivered with the dignity and self-respect that matches its grandeur, just as Allah appointed noble angels to preserve and reveal it. The Messenger and his companions should display the same character in delivering its call.
This also reveals that the Prophet chosen for receiving the revelation, and those chosen for preserving and writing it, were of the highest character and acted with complete honesty and trustworthiness. The attributes of the humans chosen on the earth to carry and communicate it matched the attributes of the angels chosen in the heavens to preserve and reveal it.

Expressing regret over human arrogance:
Although the word 'al-insan' (human) is general, the addressees are those arrogant leaders mentioned above. We have pointed to this style—showing that occasionally the discourse is directed to a specific group, but is stated in a general form to express the speaker's indifference. Here too, it is stated: 'This human is indeed a strange creature! May he be destroyed! How ungrateful he is!' His arrogance is such that he wants Allah's message to be presented to him with pleading!
'See the audacity of these short-sleeved ones!'
'Ma akfarahu' (how ungrateful he is) expresses both amazement and loathing.

This deals a blow to the pride of these arrogant individuals, but the order of themes is such that in a few brief sentences, reference is made to man's creation, the material of his creation, the stages of his creation, the means of his livelihood, life, death, the grave, and the Resurrection—showing them the transience of their pride, and proving the Resurrection and recompense through their own life.
'From what substance did He create him?' (min ayyi shay'in khalaqah) - This question is for both contempt and to draw attention to life after death. These rebellious leaders assumed that since they possess honor and status in this world, they will receive high ranks in the Hereafter. Due to this assumption, they disliked the Prophet's warning that they would be cast into Hell while those poor companions would receive Paradise and eternal kingdom. Regarding this, the Qur'an stated:
(So what is [the matter] with those who disbelieved, hastening before you, [O Muhammad], from the right and from the left, in groups? Does every person among them expect to be admitted into a garden of pleasure? No! Indeed, We have created them from what they know.) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:36-39).
Reflect on these verses in the light of their context and commentary—showing that a creature created from a drop of nesh (semen) has no right to boast of hereditary pride, assuming himself entitled to Paradise.
Similarly, the Qur'an has used the creation of man and his material of creation to prove the Resurrection—showing that the God Who can turn a drop of semen into a human being can easily resurrect him after death, as stated in Surah Al-Mu'minun:
(And certainly We created man of an extract of clay. Then We made him a small life-germ in a firm resting-place. Then We made the life-germ a clinging clot, then We made the clot a lump of flesh, then We made the lump of flesh bones, then We clothed the bones with flesh, then We caused it to grow into another creation. So blessed be Allah, the best of creators. Then indeed, after that you are to die. Then indeed, on the Day of Resurrection you will be resurrected.) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:12-16).

'From a sperm-drop He created him and proportioned him' (min-nutfatin khalaqahu fa-qaddarah) - Since the answer was obvious and no one could deny it, He answered it Himself: He created him from a sperm-drop. Elsewhere, this drop is described as 'maheen', which means insignificant and worthless. That is: it is neither significant in its quantity nor valuable; so how can it be fitting for a human created from such a drop to be arrogant!
Wisdom and planning in human creation: 'He created him and proportioned him' (khalaqahu fa-qaddarah) - Following the contempt, the discourse turns to the planning, proportioning, and easing manifest in human creation and life, which is a proof that His power did not create him in vain, but he must inevitably face a Day when His power will test his value, weigh his good and evil, and accept the worthy while casting the worthless into the Fire.
'Fa-qaddarahu' (He proportioned him) points to the stages of his gradual creation. This word is identical to what was stated regarding the phases of the moon in Surah Ya-Sin:
(And the moon—We have determined for it phases) (36:39),
pointing to man's gradual development, childhood, youth, decline, and death. This was detailed under the verses of Surah Al-Mu'minun, and is also explained in the subsequent verses. This gradual progress and planning prove His power, wisdom, and care, showing that man was created for a grand purpose—meaning he must be resurrected, accounted, and recompensed. Furthermore, this power shown in his creation proves that his recreation is not difficult.

'Then the way He eased for him' (thumma-s-sabeela yassarah) - Commentators have generally interpreted this easing as the arrangement His power made for the child to emerge from the womb, which is a reality: without this arrangement, zacha (the mother) and the child would both perish. However, in our view, there is no reason to restrict the meaning of 'sabeel' (the way) to this. Just as the child is nurtured within the womb by His power, from the cradle to the grave he covers all stages of life through His easing and guidance. It is His easing that provides milk in the mother's breasts after birth, and inspires the child to suckle. Similarly, during youth, the strength, intellect, and capabilities he displays in various fields are granted by Him. In Surah Al-A'la, He states:
(Who created and proportioned, and Who destined and [then] guided) (Al-A'la, 87:2-3).
The same is the case in the moral and spiritual world, as stated in Surah Ash-Shams:
(And [by] the soul and He who proportioned it and inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness. He has succeeded who purifies it, and he has failed who instills it [with corruption].) (Ash-Shams, 91:7-10).
In short, Allah has made the path easy for man in every aspect, both material and spiritual. If he walks on this path, he will never stumble, but by misusing his choice, he chooses the crooked path that leads to his ruin.

'Then He causes him to die and provides a grave for him' (thumma amatahu fa-aqbarah) - This points to the final stage of life: He causes him to die and provides a grave for him. Death is the most certain thing for every human. His power has bound everyone by laws from which there is no escape.
'Fa-aqbarahu' means providing a grave or burying him. This word contains a subtle indication: the one who dies does not disappear, but His power places him in the custody of the earth as a trust. What is placed as a trust must inevitably be returned one Day; so when the time comes, Allah will take this trust back from the earth.

'Then when He wills, He will resurrect him' (thumma idha sha'a ansharah) - 'Inshar' means resurrecting and bringing forth. That is: when He wills, He will resurrect him, without facing any difficulty. Since He turned a drop of semen into a human being within the womb, recreating him from the earth after burial cannot be difficult!

This verse is completely parallel to: 'May man be destroyed! How ungrateful he is!' (verse 17). Just as that verse expressed amazement at their stubbornness and argued for the Resurrection from their own creation, this verse rebukes them for their kaji (deviation) of thought, and argues for the Resurrection from the arrangement of providence spread all around them—showing that the human for whom the Lord of Majesty spread this table of blessings will not be left unaccountable; rather, a Day of Recompense is bound to come. 
'No! He has not yet accomplished what He commanded him' (kalla lamma yaqdi ma amarah) - This shows that while there is no lack of arguments and explanations, their stubbornness remains as it was. They remain heedless of the Day for which they are instructed to prepare.
'Ma amarahu' (what He commanded him) encompasses both the commands endowed within human nature, and those revealed through the prophets and messengers. Fulfilling the commands of nature is discussed under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 222: (then go in unto them as Allah has commanded you).

There is no lack of proofs for those who reflect:
That is: if they failed to understand the preceding proof, there is no lack of proofs. Let man reflect on his food, upon which his life depends—how Allah Almighty creates it, and then spreads it in various, diverse forms to suit his needs. The meaning is: if he reflects, he can easily understand that resurrecting people after death is not difficult for Allah. The rain that is the source of his food displays this daily. Furthermore, he will realize that this vast arrangement of providence requires him to be accountable before Allah; a Day must come when he is questioned whether he fulfilled the right of these blessings. Fulfilling responsibilities is a natural requirement of every right.

The way to reflect:
He did not merely invite them to reflect, but also specified the way to reflect. It is stated: it is solely by Our power and favor that We pour down water abundantly, and then split the earth open with a [spacious] splitting. Pouring down water from the heaven is beyond anyone's power, and splitting the earth open is beyond anyone's capacity, yet the productivity of the earth depends entirely on these two. This is expressed in Surah Al-Anbiya as:
(that the heavens and the earth were joined together, then We split them asunder) (Al-Anbiya, 21:30).
Keeping in view the creation of man described above, the similarity between both proofs is clear: both start by establishing the possibility of the Resurrection, and then lead to providence, accountability, and recompense. The subsequent verses will clarify this.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

Some blessings of the earth:
Through the interaction between heaven and earth—meaning rain and the earth—various blessings of providence manifest for the inhabitants of the earth. This points to some of these blessings with which the primary addressees of the Qur'an were familiar, and which are of fundamental importance for human sustenance.
First, attention is drawn to those things that grow close to the earth and do not require raising one's eyes—such as grain, grapevines, and clover. Since grain holds the primary status among food items, it is mentioned first. After grain, the second status belongs to fruits, and among fruits, grapes are of primary importance; therefore, they are mentioned as an example. Next, clover and herbage are mentioned, which serve as side-dishes to grain—either consumed raw or cooked. Although 'qadb' (clover/herbage) is used primarily for herbage consumed raw, it applies to all green vegetables.
Next, some blessings are mentioned that are obtained from lofty trees, which require raising one's eyes to see—such as olives and palm trees. The importance of olives for producing oil is well known. The Qur'an mentions the dietary importance of olive oil, and Surah An-Noor presents a metaphor of lamps lit by it.
Next, palm trees are mentioned. For the Arabs, palm trees were a source of diverse benefits and blessings: they provided dates as a nutritious fruit, served as a valuable food reserve, and also provided a delicious drink.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 27 and 28 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 27, 28, and 29 was combined in the original text.]

'And gardens of thick growth' (wa hada'iqa ghulba) - Following the specific mentions, this points to the gardens in general. 'Hadiqah' means a walled garden. 'Ghulb' is the plural of 'aghlab', which means thick-necked, but when used as an attribute of a garden, it points to the lushness of the trees, whose trunks are thick and whose branches are dense.

'And fruit and grass' (wa fakihtaw-wa abba) - This extends the scope of His blessings from humans to the animals that serve them.
While previously only specific fruits were mentioned by name, here 'fakihat' (fruit) points to all fruits—whether growing in Arabia or elsewhere, and whether superior or ordinary. 
Furthermore, while previously only those blessings were mentioned which humans enjoy, since humans depend on cattle who likewise require sustenance, this need is addressed by the addition of 'abb' (grass/herbage).
'Abb' means herbage and fresh grass. While dry fodder separated from grain also serves as food for cattle, here was the occasion to mention something that holds the same status for them as 'fakihat' (fruit) holds for humans. Fresh grass is common for them, and serves as the best substitute for all fruits, vegetables, and herbage that humans enjoy.
Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) has discussed 'abb' in detail in his commentary, proving that it is a well-known Arabic word; therefore, the reports stating that Abu Bakr and Umar did not know its meaning are incorrect. I find his research highly satisfying; refer to his commentary for details.

It is stated: We created these things as a provision for you and your cattle. The meaning is: while their being created for your benefit is clear, and it is certain that all these blessings are granted by your Lord's favor, do you reflect whether any responsibility is placed on you? Although this final question is not mentioned in words, it is clear from the context, as the primary theme is: these stubborn and arrogant leaders are denying their Lord not because His rights are not clear to them, but because they are refusing to accept anything contrary to their desires.

Reminding of the Resurrection:
'Sakhkhah' means a deafening blast or cry. Just as Surah An-Naziat used 'Tammah', this Surah uses 'Sakhkhah', pointing to the reality that the first blast of the Trumpet will be so terrifying that it will deafen the ears.
The response to the conditional 'When' (idha), as noted, is omitted based on context—meaning: today they put their fingers in their ears when the Prophet warns them, but what will they do when the Caller of God calls them from a near place with such a loud voice that it will deafen their ears!

This is the detail of that Day's terror:
'The Day a man will flee from his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his children' (yawma yafirru-l-mar'u min akheeh, wa ummihi wa abeeh, wa sahibi-tahi wa baneeh) is not the response to the condition. If it were, the style of expression would be different. The response, as noted, is omitted, but these verses throw light on it. The meaning is: today they ignore the Prophet's warning because they boast of their family and tribal unity, assuming no one can shake them; but after that deafening blast, a Day will come when everyone will be in such a state of distress that no one will help another. A brother will not listen to his brother, a son will ignore his parents, and a husband will be unable to help his wife and sons. Everyone will be so occupied with his own state that he will have no concern for another, no matter how close.
This same appeared in Surah Al-Ma'arij as:
(And no friend will ask of a friend, though they will be made to see each other. The criminal will wish that he could be ransomed from the punishment of that Day by his children, and his wife and his brother, and his nearest kindred who sheltered him, and whoever is on earth entirely, then it would save him.) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:10-14).
The difference between these two is that in the verse under discussion, the relationships are mentioned from the distant to the close, whereas in that verse, they are mentioned from the close to the distant, and both styles are highly eloquent in their respective contexts.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 34, 35, 36, and 37 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 34, 35, 36, and 37 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 34, 35, 36, and 37 was combined in the original text.]

Finally, this points to the difference that will be visible on the faces of the believers and the disbelievers on that Day, showing the state of their hearts: the hearts of the believers will be filled with hope and joy, while the hearts of the disbelievers will be filled with terror.
'Musfirah' means radiant and bright, derived from 'asfara-s-subh' (the morning brightened). This points to the first chirm of joy that will appear on the faces of the people of Paradise.
The difference that will be visible on their faces also includes 'laughing, rejoicing' (dahikatun-mustabshirah)—showing that their faces will be laughing and joyful.

The state of the disbelievers' faces is depicted as: 'And faces, that Day, upon them will be dust, blackness will cover them' (wa wujoohuy-yawma'idhin 'alayha ghabarah, tarhaquha qatarah)—showing that their faces will be covered in dust and blackness because all doors of hope will be closed to them.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 40 and 41 was combined in the original text.]
'Those are the disbelievers, the wicked' (ula'ika humu-l-kafaratu-l-fajarah) - This is their final indictment: this end will be because they chose ungratefulness and rebellion against God.
